what: Spec-judge round 1 returned ALIGNED:FAIL with a blocker: the drafted suite reverted merged PR #215, restoring the three Oracle/placement Givens to the doctrine's own worked examples and re-opening #220 -- explicitly out of scope. Root cause was not the producer: the mission branch was cut from a base predating #215, so the producer faithfully preserved the Givens of the file as it existed on that stale base. Fixed by fast-forwarding the branch onto the target and re-running the dimension surgery on top of #215's re-cut Givens. Round 2 verified mechanically: zero deleted Given/When/Scenario lines vs target.

Instrument-integrity finding beyond the CR. The ssa-lowering suite has NEVER parsed: a Feature-description line beginning with @rubric reads as a malformed tag (EPARSE line 8), present since the node was created. Every mechanical check over it fails OPEN -- check-suite prints 'suite checks OK' exit 0, gherkin-cli diff returns addOnly:true/0-changes, and the spec-gate's classify-edit-class returns NO-CONTENT-CHANGE on a rewrite of every graded scenario. A check that cannot parse its input must escalate, not exempt. Six suites repo-wide are affected (mechanic, operator, pod, recruitment, spec-anchors, ssa-lowering). This is why the node's prior spec gate could sincerely record its rubric thresholds as 'genuinely discriminating' -- no mechanical suite check has ever run here; the suite has only ever been hand-read. Parse fixed for this node in this CR; the fail-open defaults and the other five suites are separate spec-nodes, filed as follow-ups.

Clearance does not fire on this CR. PR #215 removed the @frozen tag and never restored it, so the node is currently re-opened on the target; the re-open this rework would have needed had already happened. An earlier read to the contrary came from the stale branch base, which still carried the pre-#215 frozen tag. The spec gate re-freezes the node.

Oracle-lens revert at the impl gate. The rework kept fleet-rebase-reasoned on the barrier rubric but, lifting it out of presence-grading, restated it as 'the feature missions are planned against the post-rename world' -- a requirement the doctrine does not state. Measured N=3 against the CORRECT doctrine it scored 1,1,2 (mean 1.33/2), aggregate 7.33 vs threshold 7, two of three runs exactly on threshold: a latent false-red caused by the instrument, not the subject. Unfroze the node, removed the dimension (barrier now barrier-detected+hoisted-early, threshold 5 = sum-1), and re-asserted step 2's rebase rule as a clause on the barrier boolean guard worded to the doctrine's own words. The rule is presence, not judgment -- so this applies the CR's own governing principle (presence -> boolean guard) to a defect the CR itself introduced. Rejected the alternative of adding the rule to SKILL.md: that changes the subject to fit the instrument, exceeds #221's scope, and would likely be wrong doctrine (a fence exists precisely so downstream work need NOT be planned against the post-rename world).

Cold re-gate returned architect-spec FAIL: two README 'Where the margin is thin' bullets still described the reverted three-dimension barrier rubric (3+3+2 at threshold 7, aggregate 7.33) after the .feature had moved to two dimensions at threshold 5. Same defect class as the CR fixes -- prose and instrument disagreeing -- and this README drives the impl gate via pre-registered actions, so false numbers there actively misdirect. Reconciled: barrier's margin restated as reasoned-not-measured, and the suite's only ever-measured variance re-described as instrument error rather than signal.

Calibration anchor did not replicate. threshold = sum(max)-1 was calibrated on a single measurement of catches-misalignment at 2.33/3 against a correct doctrine; a later N=3 run measured 3.00/3 with zero variance. The anchor is now a sample of two disagreeing measurements. sum-1 is retained (it still binds -- the floor, not the threshold, is what must be near zero -- and the added fold makes slack more valuable, not less), but the README no longer reports the slack as evidence-backed. Also recorded: the impl-judge found catches-misalignment scored at ceiling with judges citing the doctrine's own structurally-isomorphic worked example -- absorption (#220/#222 territory), which is #221's DECLARED limitation, not a refutation: this CR raises the bar against a sloppy doctrine, never a memorizing one.

DISCRIMINATION MEASURED (42 producer runs, 60 judge runs, every cell N=3). Two-stage design so the rubric is the only variable: a producer saw only a doctrine version + the scenario Given/When and wrote a plan; a separate cold aced-case-judge saw only that plan + one rubric version. OLD and NEW Given/When are byte-identical, so the SAME plan was scored against both rubrics. RESULT: rubric-level discrimination SUPPORTED. Cell D2 (over-merge mutant): the same over-merged plan scores OLD 3+3+2=8 >= 6 PASS 3/3, NEW regroup-by-ownership 3 + disjoint-nodes-not-fused 0 = 3 < 5 FAIL 3/3 -- clean, zero variance, exactly the predicted mechanism. Cell C (no-relax mutant): penetrated only 1 of 6 runs, but on that run NEW 3 < 4 FAIL vs OLD 6 >= 6 PASS at the old threshold boundary -- real but thin (N=1). CONTROLS HOLD: the real doctrine passes 3/3 on stale, barrier, low-confidence, regroup (misaligned 2/3, see the #249 contradiction, not a rubric defect). The old suite could not register either miss.

METHOD FINDING -- penetration is the hidden variable in mutation-testing a rubric. 3 of 4 conductor-supplied mutants failed to induce the degraded BEHAVIOR they were meant to; a mutation that does not change behavior measures nothing about a rubric. Mutant D was inert (the mutated clause's own antecedent scoped the over-merge to the shared node, and step 3's single-writer rule plus step 2's do-not-fuse rule pinned correct behavior anyway) -- the runner built and disclosed its own mutantD2 to reach the dimension. Mutant A barely penetrated because steps 1/2/6 still instruct recording shown-work, so producers kept emitting evidence: a risk a prior runner explicitly warned the conductor about and the conductor failed to act on before dispatch. Mutant B's OLD dimension did not degrade either (fleet-rebase-reasoned scored 2/2), so B discriminates only via the NEW boolean guard. Verify mutant PENETRATION before scoring, or the experiment silently measures nothing -- the same fail-open shape as #243.

Absorption data (out of scope, #220/#222 territory, reported not acted on). Judges structurally could not absorb -- they never saw the doctrine, only plan + rubric. Absorption appears at the PRODUCER stage and is strong: in the two cells where producers were asked to invent their own CR specifics, 6/6 reached for the doctrine's own worked examples (mailer retry, telemetry tracker, rate-limiter, audit-log) as their invented change requests. Where the scenario supplied its own domain, absorption had no room to show. Confirms #221's declared caveat: this CR raises the bar against a sloppy doctrine, never a memorizing one.

Second evidence-driven revert. The impl gate ran all 19 scenarios N=3 (real doctrine PASSED, 66/66 dimension-scores at ceiling) and then ran its own ABLATION study -- delete a rule, re-score blind. barrier 6.00->3.67 FAIL 3/3; misaligned 6.00->2.33 FAIL 2/3; irreducible 5.00->3.33 FAIL 2/3; cohesion 3.00->3.00 PASS 3/3, delta 0.00 with the cohesion rule deleted WHOLE. cohesion-preserved was UNLOSEABLE -- judges scored it off single-writer, because the Given confines work to one spec-node and single-writer already guarantees one node lands in one mission. The #221 defect class surviving inside the CR that removes it. Demoted to an untagged boolean guard (an unloseable assertion is what a guard is for); padding rejected. All 10 remaining rubrics now carry 2 independently-loseable dimensions at sum(max)-1 with NO exceptions, so the feature description's universal claim is finally true of itself. A real cohesion probe needs a Given planting an over-merge temptation -> #250. METHOD FINDING: the conductor's pre-registered trigger ('cohesion mean below 3.0 -> demote') could NEVER FIRE -- 3.00 was the dimension's FLOOR, not a pass, so the condition presumed the loseability in doubt. A measured ceiling is not evidence a dimension works; it is equally consistent with a dimension that cannot fail. Test loseability by ABLATING the rule and re-scoring, never by watching the mean against a correct subject.

Cold re-gate returned architect-spec FAIL: the demotion deleted the cohesion exception from the feature description but left the round-1 comment block above the scenario asserting 'Single dimension by design, so this threshold is forced to max' -- every load-bearing claim false of the scenario it annotates, asserting as design intent the exact position the README documents as disproven by ablation, with a pointer at the section that refutes it. Third instance of this defect class in one mission (after SKILL.md's stale grading claim and the README's stale barrier numbers): derived prose outliving the contract it describes. Rewritten to state the entailment and cite #250.

Scoped re-verification PASSED 3/3 on all three changed scenarios, and the Decision-evidence fix took (barrier guard 2/3 -> 3/3, with all three runs placing the sequencing fact in the exact Architect-verdict bullet edited). But the cold judge caught a real error in the conductor's own follow-up: the #250 issue, the .feature comment, and the README all said grading cohesion needs an OVER-MERGE temptation. Backwards. Cohesion is the OVER-SPLIT rule; its miss is scattering one node into fragments. An over-merge probe duplicates disjoint-nodes-not-fused (already graded on regroup) AND would not de-entail the assertion, since single-writer forces the coupled node into one mission however many other nodes exist. As filed, #250 would have built a duplicate dimension and left cohesion unloseable -- the defect surviving a second time. Corrected in all three places; #250 annotated. Also recorded from the same run: the barrier guard's clause 2 is vacuous on its own Given (one CR wholly the refactor -> a single-mission partition -> 'no other lowered mission starts before the barrier retires' is vacuously true), pre-existing from #189, same unloseable-assertion family, folded into #250.

Honesty bound on the determinism claim, per the impl-judge: N=3 cannot distinguish p=1.0 from p=0.9 -- the prior 2/3 was drawn from the same sample size, so 3/3 is consistent with a residual ~10% omission rate. The barrier guard's Decision-evidence fix is SUPPORTED, not PROVEN. Recorded rather than reported as determinism.
